entry.title: Attacking Insecure Deserialization in .NET — Part 2: Formatters and .NET Commons Gadgets
Attacking Insecure Deserialization in .NET — Part 1: Fundamentals and Lab Setup
entry.content:
Introduction

This article introduces Insecure Deserialization attacks in .NET and explains how attackers abuse gadget chains to achieve remote code execution.

We will explore:

  • The fundamentals of .NET deserialization
  • Gadget chains and serializers
  • Tools such as ysoserial.net
  • How to build a vulnerable ASP.NET lab for testing

Insecure Deserialization in .NET – Definition, Key Concepts, Tools, and Exploitation Flow

Definition

This vulnerability occurs when an application deserializes untrusted data without proper validation. -> when the target application uses vulnerable libraries, framework classes, or built-in platform classes that can be abused during deserialization.

During deserialization, the application reconstructs objects from serialized data. In some cases, special methods (such as constructors or deserialization callbacks like OnDeserialized) may be automatically executed.

If an attacker can control the serialized data, they may craft a malicious object that triggers unintended code execution during the deserialization process.

A vulnerable class that can be abused during deserialization is called a gadget.

When multiple gadgets are chained together so their method calls lead to arbitrary code execution, the sequence is known as a gadget chain.

Key Concepts

  • Gadget and Gadget Chain
  • Serializers / Formatters

Gadget:

  • A legitimate framework class that can be abused during deserialization to execute unintended behavior.

Gadget chain:

  • One gadget may not give RCE directly.
  • So attackers chain multiple gadgets:
  • Object A → triggers B → calls C → executes Process.Start()
  • Tools like ysoserial.net generate these chains automatically.
File Purpose
Gadget A specific class (or chain of classes) inside a library that can be abused
Gadget chain Multiple gadgets combined to reach RCE

Where do gadgets come from

They usually come from:

  • Third-party libraries
  • Framework classes
  • Built-in platform classes

For example:

  • commons-collections (in Java)
  • .NET framework classes
  • ASP.NET components
  • Logging libraries

Serializers / Deserializers:

  • A serializer converts an object → data.
  • A deserializer converts data → object.

So, the concept is:

  • object → JSON → object

Formatters:

In old .NET architecture, a formatter is a type of serializer/deserializer designed to convert objects to a specific format.

Example formatters:

Formatter Output format
BinaryFormatter binary
SoapFormatter SOAP XML
LosFormatter ASP.NET ViewState

So:

  • Serializer = generic concept
  • Formatter = specific .NET serializer implementation

Tools

ysoserial.net

A tool used to generate malicious serialized payloads for testing .NET deserialization vulnerabilities.

  • Contains a collection of known gadget chains (e.g., TypeConfuseDelegate, ActivitySurrogateSelector, ObjectDataProvider).
  • Generates serialized objects that embed a command inside the payload.
  • Used for security testing and research of insecure deserialization.

Key points:

  • It generates serialized payloads
  • It relies on existing gadget chains to trigger code execution when deserialized.
  • It embeds a command string inside serialized object

See: https://github.com/pwntester/ysoserial.net

Exploitation (Attack flow)

During deserialization:

  1. The application reconstructs objects from user input
  2. Some objects execute code automatically during construction or property initialization
  3. If the attacker controls the object data, this behavior can be abused

If the attacker controls both the object type and its data, a gadget chain may execute dangerous functionality.

Example:

A gadget might:

  • Accept method name
  • Accept parameters
  • Invoke method during deserialization
  • If attacker sets Method = "Start" Parameter = "cmd.exe"
  • Boom → RCE.

Why do gadgets exist in large frameworks?

Most large frameworks contain gadgets because:

  • They were not designed with deserialization attacks in mind
  • Many classes contain dynamic behaviors such as reflection, callbacks, or event handlers

When does this attack become possible?

An insecure deserialization attack becomes possible when:

  • A gadget exists
  • Gadget executes something during deserialization
  • That something can be influenced by attacker

In old .NET Framework, gadgets such as ObjectDataProvider, TypeConfuseDelegate, WindowsIdentity were commonly abused.

In modern environments such as .NET 6+:

  • Exploitation is generally more difficult
  • Many insecure serializers are deprecated or restricted
  • Successful attacks often require vulnerable third-party libraries
  • Or custom application code performing unsafe deserialization

Exploitation Conditions & Prerequisites

Exploit prerequisites:

  • A vulnerable deserialization endpoint
  • The right gadget chain matching the target’s libraries

Preparing our ASP.NET Lab to Test Deserialization Attacks on .NET

ASP.NET is a web framework developed by Microsoft. It is used to build, Web applications, APIs, Enterprise systems, and Internal company portals.

Installing .NET SDK on linux

First you have to update your system with sudo apt update and sudo apt upgrade.

Install the SDK:

Verify installation:

Create a Test ASP.NET Web App

Create a project folder:

Create a new web app:

This generates an ASP.NET Core Razor application.

Run the .NET webserver locally:

You should see something like:

Open browser http://localhost:5000 or testing locally from Burpsuite, we can run the server like that:

Then intercept traffic using burpsuite.

Now we can create simple vulnerable endpoints in ASP.NET and test using:

  • Newtonsoft.Json
  • BinaryFormatter
  • DataContractSerializer
  • ObjectStateFormatter

Create minimal ASP.NET web app

Create template for a minimal ASP.NET web application (no Razor pages, just a simple HTTP server):

Project Structure - Overview

Explanation:

File Purpose
Program.cs Main application code
webapp.csproj Project configuration
appsettings.json Application settings
Properties/ launch settings
obj/ build artifacts

Viewing the main application file

Program.cs:

That’s will:

  • Creates an endpoint /
  • Returns “Hello World!” on that endpoint

Running the application: dotnet run --urls=http://0.0.0.0:5000 and then curl:

Now we can create endpoints like /deserialize, /api/test, etc. for testing our .NET deserialization attacks and web vulnerabilities.

We gonna see a small vulnerable .NET endpoint I’ll add it to Program.cs to test insecure deserialization.

Hack The Box Machines Related to Deserialization Attacks

The following Hack The Box machines contains vulnerabilities and techniques related to .NET and Java deserialization attacks, gadget chains, or cryptographic key exploitation (e.g., ViewState / MachineKey abuse).

Machine Technology Topic
Json Java Java deserialization
Atlas ASP.NET ViewState / MachineKey exploitation
Sharp .NET .NET application exploitation
Visual ASP.NET ViewState / MachineKey abuse
Scrambled Java Java deserialization

entry.title: HackTheBox - GiveBack (Linux/Medium)
entry.content:
Enumeration

Privilege Escalation: Exploit runc
